sections.paragraph:
with suff. הֶבְלִי, pl. הֲבָלִים constr. הַבְלֵי.

(1) breath, breathing, used of a gentle breeze, Isa 57:13. (Well rendered by the Vulg. aura. Less correctly by the LXX. καταιγίς.) More often used of the breath of the mouth (Kimchi, איד שיצא מפה. Aqu. ἀτμίς, Symm. ἀτμός, which word, Sap. vii. 25, Syr. is rendered ܗܶܒܥܳ). Commonly used of any thing transitory, evanescent, frail. Job 7:16, כִּי הֶבֶל יָמַי “for my days are a breath;” Pro 13:11, הֹון מֵהֶבֶל יִמְעַט “riches vanish more quickly than a breath;” Ecc 11:10, “childhood and youth are vanity;” Pro 21:6 31:30 Psa 39:6 Ecc 1:2, 14 2:11 , 17 , 23 4:4 , 5:9 6:9 , etc. Hence arises the signification something vain and empty, Lam 4:17 Jer 10:3, Jer 10:8, and adv. vainly, emptily, in vain; Job 9:29 21:34 35:16 Isa 30:7 Psa 39:7. Specially used of idols as being vain and impotent, also used of their worship, 2Ki 17:15 Jer 2:5. Plur. Psa 31:7, הַבְלֵי שָׁוְא “vain idols.” Jon 2:9.

(2) exhalation, vapour, mist, darkness, which cannot be seen through. Ecc 6:4, of an abortion; “for it comes in a mist, and goes away in vanity,” seen by no one; Ecc 11:8, כָּל־שֶׁבָּא הֶבֶל “all that is coming is a mist,” i.e. involved in darkness; Ecc 8:14.

(3) pr.n. Abel (LXX. Ἄβελ), the second son of Adam; prob. so called from the shortness of his life [but he had this name from his birth]; Gen 4:2, seq.
